speaker: interviewer
text: --Moore.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. Now we-- we came as a group. We went from Czechoslovakia back to Germany. And our first station was Munich, Germany. In Munich, I took all my German marks that I had with me, and I changed it into dollars. So I had just a few dollars from a lot of German marks. But I had dollars. And they were worth a lot of money.

speaker: interviewee
text: So with these dollars, I was in Munich. And it's-- and I found out that a lot of my friends that were in camp with me were in Frankfurt am Main. So I went to Frankfurt am Main.

speaker: interviewee
text: And in Frankfurt am Main, I was in Frankfurt am Main for a little while. And I met a Polish officer who I got acquainted with, who somehow introduced himself to me. And we became friendly. And he asked me if I would be interested to go to Paris with him, if I had money to buy something in Germany so I could sell in Paris-- and the prices in Paris are tenfold.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I trusted him. And he took me to the Polish headquarters-- the Polish military headquarters in Frankfurt am Main. That was in 1946 in January. And they gave me a complete custom made outfit for me as a Polish officer. I was an officer. I was a lieutenant in the Polish army wearing an American uniform with the side saying Poland.

speaker: interviewee
text: And he had all the legitimate papers, because he was a real officer. And all the papers listing me with my name-- Polish name, date of birth, everything. And we went from Frankfurt. I bought certain things in Frankfurt in order to sell it in Paris. And we went on the-- on the American train that went directly from Frankfurt all the way to Paris. No problems. And then arriving in Paris, he-- we went to a certain area where he knew hotels. And we registered in a hotel.

speaker: interviewee
text: So he left me in Paris. And he went to Belgium. But he told me where to go in Paris, where the Jewish neighborhood is. He said that once you go to a Jewish neighborhood, you'll have no problem. You'll find things for yourself.

speaker: interviewee
text: He try-- he figured that if I had money to buy stuff in Germany, I'll find my way through the Paris. That's exactly the way it was. When I went into the Jewish neighborhood, I found guys. And I sold what I had with me. And I got a substantial amount of money. Well, I waited. We had a ten-day furlough in Paris. We were supposed to go back to Frankfurt. That was our idea. Ten days later, he was supposed to pick me up in Paris to go back to Frankfurt am Main. But he never showed up.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I wore the uniform. I went to Champs Elysees. I walked on Champs Elysees. And all the Polish soldiers salute me. I am an officer! [LAUGHING]. Interesting! Then I went back to the Jewish neighborhood. And I got acquainted with a man who has a factory-- a clothing factory. And he had dinner in that restaurant. And I had dinner in that restaurant. And we start talking. And I ask him, how do you stay in Paris? Now that this guy doesn't come back, what do I do?

speaker: interviewee
text: He says, don't worry about it. I'll give you a contract. You'll work for me for a year. And you don't have to work, but I'll give you the contract. And with this contract, you go to the [FRENCH], you know, the city hall. And if you have a contract to work, they give you a permit to stay in Paris. That's exactly the way it was. Now I'm a legitimate citizen of Paris. So I shed my uniform. And I bought some civilian clothes because I had money. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And then I met-- I met some young Jewish guys. And they told me what to do. They said, listen, you have to learn French. Otherwise it's very hard to live here unless you speak French. So they-- they took me to some kind of organization that had the Eiffel family chateau to the disposition of the Jewish Family Service in Paris. And that particular chateau contained only Jewish boys from Poland, and from France, and Romania, and Czechoslovakia, and Hungary-- boys that were in Paris that have no family. And all of us were young, single men. And they enabled us. Every day, we stayed there in the chateau. They gave us food, lodging, no money, no charge. They gave us money for transportation. And we had to go to the L'ecole L'alliance Francais to learn French. And in six weeks, I was speaking French. I had no problem. So I left that chateau. And I rented a hotel. And I went back to Germany. And I bought merchandise, brought it again back as a civilian with different papers-- everything legitimate because I was a resident of Paris. So that went on for me for about three years.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what were those years? From 1946--

speaker: interviewee
text: From 1946 to 1949. Then in 1949, the borders became a little bit more strict. You had to have more official papers. You have to have visas, you know. Everybody regained their-- their authority. The German area was not like occupied anymore. They had their own government, and their-- their own border patrols. So instead of going directly on a train to France, and having just one border patrol, we had two border patrols. Now I had a German border patrol and a French border control. It gets to be more complicated.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I decided that I want to leave, go to America. So I had registered in Germany to go to America. And I waited like about three months. And I came to America in 1949 in October-- about November-- in the end of October, beginning November of 1949.

speaker: interviewee
text: I came to a city called Modesto, California. They-- they sponsored me. They only were looking for single Jewish boys. And I was a single Jewish boy.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I was in Modesto for three months. I couldn't speak English. So in Modesto, they wanted me to go to school. But I was-- I was of the opinion that I'm too old to go to school. So all I wanted is a job. And they gave me a-- I was-- I-- I got a job by a Jewish man that has a jewelry store in Modesto.

speaker: interviewee
text: And he gave me a job, paid me $25 a week. And with this $25 a week, I was going to school at night to learn English. And I-- and they gave me lodging. So the $25 was just for food. I managed pretty well. Then six weeks later, it was in January of 1950. I talked to the people of Modesto. And I told them that I speak English well enough to go to a big city. Because I am from a big city. And I-- I couldn't live in a little community. And they agreed with me.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I went to Los Angeles. And I got myself a job in a rubber stamp company in Los Angeles. And the first three days I was in the city, I got a job. And the going rate for the minimum wage was $0.65 an hour. So when-- when I got a job setting type as barely speaking English, the company offered me $1.35 an hour. And I was very happy, because it was unbelievable money. And then about two weeks later, they say, we'll give you a raise to $1.65. Plus we give you all the overtime you can handle.

speaker: interviewee
text: I was very happy. I was all alone, was a single man. [COUGHS] Then three months after I was in Los Angeles, I bought a car. I bought a car for cash, $330. [INAUDIBLE]. I was right away a rich man. Anyways, and I met my wife in the meantime. And we got married in December of 1950. And in December, 1950, I still worked as a-- setting type for a little while. Then I went with my wife buying a pair of shoes. And I looked at that shoe business. And I said, you know, I like-- I says, I like that. So I decided to look into it. And eventually I got a job as a shoe salesman. And a few weeks later, I was the manager of a shoe store. And after a certain time being a manager of a shoe store, I opened my own shoe store. So at one time during the-- my shoe business career, I had the three shoe stores in Los Angeles. And I was married. Children were born. I had one son born in 1953, and another son born in 1957, and a third son was born in 1960. They all live in Los Angeles. Two of them are married. And thank God, I have seven grandchildren. And going back a little while-- this is 1990-- my wife got sick. And she died in March of 1990. I was widowed then. By [? but ?] that time, my children were grown and I had seven grandchildren. I was established. And I had a new business. And-- and the end of '92, I was lucky enough to get acquainted with a lady that is happen to be my wife at the present. And her name is Rita Lowy Moore. And we're married. And we know each for about three years. But we've married about a year and a half. And she is a member of my family, as well as her family is a member of our family. Two families got together. And we just like each other so much. It's almost like loving each other. Especially the grandchildren and my children are in love with her. That's for sure. And her children have a wonderful relationship with me.

speaker: interviewer
text: That's good.

speaker: interviewee
text: So this is my story.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you're still in the construction business at this point.

speaker: interviewee
text: I am right now, I am in the construction business. For the last 12 years, I was in the-- I-- since 1983, I am in the construction-- home construction-- reconstruction business-- with a partner. And I'm very happy doing what I do. And I'm still working. And this is [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] September 1995.

speaker: interviewer
text: How has-- how did your Holocaust experience sort of shape the way you look at life?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, the Holocaust experience taught me that life is just destiny, destiny. And every one of us is destined for a different experience. And my experience is like I have told you my story, that this was probably my destiny. It's a Jewish-- we say it's [NON-ENGLISH]. And each one of us has a different destiny.

speaker: interviewer
text: And did--?

speaker: interviewee
text: Is it-- because you have to be in the right place at the right time and have a little luck? You can call it that. Everyone calls it differently. But basically, this is what it is. I was in the right place at the right time, and I survived.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you remain religious? Have you remained religious?

speaker: interviewee
text: I am-- I had many years that I wasn't religious at all. But when my children came of the age of going to school, I decided to become religious again-- mostly because of them. Because I didn't want them not to have the experiences that I had. At least they know about it. And all my children went to yeshiva. So I have one, my middle son, went to yeshiva in the beginning, and then he didn't want to go. So he went to public school. And my oldest son went to yeshiva. And he graduated the yeshiva school in Los Angeles at Rabbi [? Vasoven's ?] yeshiva on the Westside. And he's a highly educated person. He has a PhD in psychology. And my middle son became a CPA. And my youngest son went to Hebrew school all his life-- except for the universities. He graduated Rambam in the west side-- Rambam High School. He went to university-- Yeshiva University New York.

speaker: interviewee
text: He went to yeshiva in San Jose and the-- and the University of San Jose. And he went to University of San Diego. And he went to College of Optometry in Fullerton, right? [GLANCING AT WIFE] And he's now a practicing doctor in-- in San Fernando Valley. He is married. He has three children.

speaker: interviewee
text: And my oldest son is married. And he has four children. So I am a grandpa. And I have a brand new grandma for my grandson-- children.

speaker: interviewer
text: Would you say that having your family-- your children and you wife-- your two wives, one after the other, helped you fill the-- the emptiness that you felt losing your family so early? You-- you lost everyone. [INTERPOSING VOICES]

speaker: interviewee
text: I lost everybody my family. And I build my own family, I created a family brand new. And I feel good about it. Because I was all alone. I'm not alone, and thank God,

speaker: interviewee
text: And my wife of 40 years, that was my partner. It's just one of those destinies that she is not with us. But thank God, like we say in Jewish. And in Jewish, we always say thank God. Even the non-believers say thank God. True, isn't it? So I have a wife. And thank God that I'm happy. OK, good. Thank you very much. That's good. I will bring-- [INAUDIBLE] OK. Rita, would you like to give us your full name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, all the way through-- Rita [? Panstor ?] was my maiden name. Then I was Rita [? Panstor-Lowy. ?] Now I'm Rita [? Panstor-Lowy-Moore ?]

speaker: interviewer
text: I see, OK. And I'd like to ask you how it has been for you living with a survivor?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, when Maurice told me his story, I really couldn't believe all that he had gone through. He's such an amazing person. He loves life. He knows how to live life. And he has made me appreciate even more what I have and be thankful for having met him. Because he's just added so much to-- to our existence with each other.

speaker: interviewer
text: [INAUDIBLE] Is there anything else you'd like to say, Rita, before I ask Maurice another question?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I think this is-- this is his life. And all I can say is for everything he's been through, he's just remarkable. Like I don't know how other survivors are, personally. My first husband was also a survivor, but he was very young at the time. But to come through all that he did and-- and love life the way he does, it's really wonderful.

speaker: interviewer
text: Maurice, you did talk about how you believe that a lot of what happened to you was destiny. Is there anything else that you would like to say to future generations based on the Holocaust?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, destiny is also something that you make. You make it happen. You have to believe in yourself. And life is very precious, because we only have one time around. So when we respect life, and the things that were given to us, it's a miracle in itself.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, well thank you very much.

speaker: interviewee
text: This document shows that I was a prisoner in Auschwitz. And I was-- I arrived to Auschwitz on December-- in the month of December 1942. I was taken out of Auschwitz from a subcamp of Auschwitz to Buchenwald in January of 1945. And this is a statement from the now-governing-- governing office of the museum in Auschwitz in Poland.

speaker: interviewer
text: [INAUDIBLE]

speaker: interviewee
text: This picture is taken care of me in 1946. And the reason for that picture is mainly because the tie that I wear was given to me by an American soldier in Frankfurt, Germany. So that I remember. And the picture is here to prove it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you say that you had that tie for a long time?

speaker: interviewee
text: I had it for about 45 years. This picture was taken of me in a studio by a professional photographer in 1945, somehow about the end of '45. Not bad.

speaker: interviewer
text: Go.

speaker: interviewee
text: This picture depicts a memorial to the ghetto fighters in Warsaw, which is built on the area where I used to live before the war. That street does not exist anymore as a functioning street, except it's a memorial park in the city of Warsaw.

speaker: interviewer
text: Go.

speaker: interviewee
text: This picture is part of the memorial to the ghetto fighters of Warsaw depicting the leader. And his name is Anielewicz. He was killed in the fighting in the Warsaw ghetto--

speaker: interviewer
text: You said--

speaker: interviewer
text: Maurice, you said that when you went back to Warsaw, it was not the same as before.

speaker: interviewee
text: The streets of Warsaw were rebuilt entirely differently. And the people that were in Warsaw for many years could not find their way, so differently rebuilt is the city. This is the gates in Auschwitz that I came through in 1942 in December-- the same gates, the same camp that I revisited in 1995, to the 50th anniversary of the liberation.

speaker: interviewer
text: And the sign above the gate-- the sign above the gate says? The sign above the gate says 'Arbeit Macht Frei--' work keeps you free.

speaker: interviewee
text: This building depicts the barracks that I was taken to in 1942 in Auschwitz, waiting to be taken to work. I spent there ten days in the month of December in 1942, luckily so for me. This picture depicts my first wife. Her name was Dollie. And we were married for 40 years. This picture was taken approximately in 19-- I would say-- '66, '65, '66. This picture shows my wife, my present wife-- God love her. And I love her. And we're together now for about 18 months. And we hope to be together the rest of our lives.

speaker: interviewee
text: Tell me when. OK, this picture to the left represents my wife and I and my three sons. And to the left is Ira. He is my middle son. Next to him, to his right, is Jan, Dr. Jan Moore, and myself and my wife, and then Dr. Brian Moore, my oldest son. And this was during your wedding?

speaker: interviewee
text: And this was during our wedding.

speaker: interviewer
text: And the date again?

speaker: interviewee
text: May 29th.

speaker: interviewee
text: May 29th--

speaker: interviewee
text: Right.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, May 29th, '94. This picture depicts-- the elderly lady is my wife's mother. And her-- her name is [? Rae ?] [? Panster. ?] Next to her is my daughter-in-law Ann-- my son Brian's wife. Next to her is the-- the lady. Her name is-- my daughter-in-law. She's-- she's Lori. And next to Lori is my granddaughter staying next to my wife. Her name is Jessica. My wife's name is Rita. Next to Rita is my son Ira. And then next to Ira, a little bit to the right, is Brian, my oldest son. And right in front of him, next to myself is my son Jan. Next to me, standing to my right, is my grandson--

speaker: interviewee
text: Richard.

speaker: interviewee
text: --Richard. And next to him, the young man is my grandson Jason, standing up. Now we're going to the girls. The girl next to Jason is Shelley. She is between two boys. And the little girl-- and to the left is-- what was her name? [INAUDIBLE] We're going to get back to her. And the little boy, his name is Andrew, sitting with a funny face. Next to Andrew to the right is Mirasha. And the little girl's name is--

speaker: interviewee
text: Dvora.

speaker: interviewee
text: Divora! [LAUGHTER] Now we got them all! [LAUGHTER]
